questions.stem: According to Fowler's definition, what is refactoring?

options.text: A change made to the internal structure of software to make it easier to understand and cheaper to modify, without changing observable behaviour
A change that adds new features while keeping the existing tests green
An optimisation that improves runtime performance by restructuring algorithms
A rewrite of a module from scratch to remove accumulated technical debt

questions.explanation: Fowler defines refactoring as a change to the internal structure to make software easier to understand and cheaper to modify, with observable behaviour unchanged. B is wrong because refactoring adds no functionality. C confuses refactoring with performance tuning; the goal is understandability/modifiability, not speed. D is a rewrite, not a behaviour-preserving restructuring. (Refactoring1 p.3)

questions.stem: In Fowler's definition of refactoring, what must NOT change?

options.text: The number of classes in the system
The observable behaviour of the software
The internal structure of the software
The names of methods and variables

questions.explanation: The defining constraint of refactoring is that observable behaviour must not change. C is exactly what refactoring DOES change (internal structure). D and A are commonly altered during refactoring (renaming, extracting/inlining classes). (Refactoring1 p.3)

questions.stem: A developer restructures a method's internals and, in the process, a previously-correct output value becomes different. Why is this NOT a refactoring?

options.text: Because the change was not verified by automated tests
Because refactoring is only allowed during the Postfactoring phase
Because the observable behaviour changed, so by definition it is a modification, not a refactoring
Because refactoring may only be applied to methods shorter than 15 lines

questions.explanation: By definition refactoring preserves observable behaviour; if an output changes it is a functional modification, not a refactoring. D invents a length rule that does not gate the definition. A is good practice but a missing test does not change the definition. B is false; refactoring happens in both Prefactoring and Postfactoring. (Refactoring1 p.3)

questions.stem: Which pair correctly states the two goals of refactoring named in Fowler's definition?

options.text: Faster to execute and smaller in memory
Easier to understand and cheaper to modify

questions.explanation: Fowler's definition names two human-facing goals: easier to understand and cheaper to modify. A is performance, not a refactoring goal. B adds features/fixes bugs which are not refactoring's defining goals. C names documentation/portability which are not in the definition. (Refactoring1 p.3)

questions.stem: The lab restates refactoring as 'a series of small ___ transformations'. Which word fills the blank?
questions.explanation: The lab describes refactoring as a series of small behavior-preserving transformations. The other options describe activities (performance tuning, feature addition, type checking) that are explicitly not what refactoring is. (RefactLab p.1)

questions.stem: Why does keeping each refactoring step small matter, according to the lab?

options.text: Small steps avoid the need to run any tests
Each small step is less likely to go wrong and the system is kept fully working after each one
Small steps are required by the SIG 15-line guideline
Small steps execute faster than large rewrites

questions.explanation: The lab notes a single refactoring does little, but a sequence produces significant restructuring; because each step is small it is less likely to go wrong and the system stays working after each. C misattributes the rationale to a metric. D is about runtime, irrelevant. A is the opposite of good practice. (RefactLab p.1)

questions.stem: Kerievsky's phrase 'the algebra of refactoring' most directly refers to which idea?

options.text: Computing man-months from lines of code
Measuring fan-in to assess module coupling
Solving design problems through sequences of small behaviour-preserving steps
Calculating cyclomatic complexity from branch points

questions.explanation: Kerievsky calls patterns the 'word problems' and refactoring the 'algebra' — solving design problems via behaviour-preserving steps. D, A, and B are SIG/BetterCode measurement formulas, a different deck's concern, not the algebra metaphor. (HighLevelRefactoring p.10)

questions.stem: A colleague claims 'I refactored the parser and it now runs twice as fast — that was the whole point.' Which correction is most accurate?

options.text: Refactoring must always make code faster, so this is a textbook example
Refactoring is only valid if it reduces lines of code below 15
Refactoring is only valid if it removes a design pattern
Speed is not the goal of refactoring; the goal is understandability and modifiability, and behaviour (including outputs) must be preserved

questions.explanation: Refactoring targets understandability/modifiability, not runtime speed; a speedup may be a side effect but is not the point, and behaviour must stay the same. A is the opposite of the definition. B and C invent constraints that are not part of the definition. (Refactoring1 p.3)

questions.stem: Which of the following is one of Fowler's four reasons to refactor?

options.text: Refactoring improves the design of software
Refactoring guarantees the absence of bugs
Refactoring eliminates the need for tests

questions.explanation: Fowler's four reasons are: improves design, makes software easier to understand, helps find bugs, helps you program faster. 'Improves the design' is one of them. B overstates ('helps find' bugs, not guarantees none). C (compile time) and D (no tests) are not among the reasons. (Refactoring1 p.4)

questions.stem: Fowler argues refactoring 'helps you find bugs.' What is the mechanism?

options.text: Refactoring rewrites the failing test cases
Clarifying the structure surfaces hidden defects
Refactoring runs a static analyser that flags every bug

questions.explanation: Fowler's point is that clarifying structure makes hidden defects visible. C and D describe tools/actions Fowler does not claim refactoring performs; refactoring itself does not run analysers or insert assertions. A is false and would undermine the tests' value. (Refactoring1 p.4)

questions.stem: Which of Fowler's four 'why refactor' reasons is described as counter-intuitive but central?

options.text: Refactoring makes software easier to understand
Refactoring improves the design of software

questions.explanation: Fowler highlights 'helps you program faster' as counter-intuitive — spending time restructuring seems to slow you, but good design speeds future work. The other three are also reasons but are not framed as the counter-intuitive one. (Refactoring1 p.4)

questions.stem: Fowler notes code is 'read far more than it is written.' Which of his four reasons does this support?

options.text: Refactoring improves the design of software
Refactoring makes software easier to understand

questions.explanation: Because code is read more than written, refactoring for understandability pays off — this directly supports 'makes software easier to understand.' The other reasons are valid but not the one this observation underpins. (Refactoring1 p.4)

questions.stem: Which statement is NOT one of Fowler's four reasons to refactor?

options.text: Refactoring guarantees the software meets all functional requirements
Refactoring improves the design of software
Refactoring makes software easier to understand

questions.explanation: The four reasons are: improves design, easier to understand, helps find bugs, program faster. 'Guarantees requirements are met' is not among them — refactoring is behaviour-preserving and adds no requirement satisfaction. The other three are genuine reasons. (Refactoring1 p.4)

questions.stem: What does Fowler's 'Rule of Three' say?

options.text: A class may have at most three responsibilities
Refactor the third time you do something similar
A method may have at most three parameters

questions.explanation: The Rule of Three: the first time just do it, the second wince and duplicate, the third time refactor. C invents a commit cadence. D and A are unrelated metric-style rules not stated by the Rule of Three. (Refactoring1 p.5)

questions.stem: The Rule of Three is described as the canonical antidote to which code smell?
questions.explanation: The Rule of Three tells you to refactor when you have repeated something a third time — the canonical antidote to Duplicated Code. The other smells are addressed by different refactorings (e.g. Introduce Parameter Object, Move Method, Inline Class). (Refactoring1 p.5)

questions.stem: Fowler's 'Refactor When You Add Function' trigger maps to which change-process phase in this course?
questions.explanation: Cleaning up so a new feature fits is refactoring before the functional change — Prefactoring. Postfactoring is the after-cleanup. Actualization is where the new behaviour is actually added. Concept Location is finding where to change, an earlier phase. (Refactoring1 p.5)

questions.stem: Which is one of Fowler's three 'when to refactor' triggers (besides the Rule of Three)?

options.text: When you need to fix a bug

questions.explanation: Fowler's triggers are: add function, fix a bug, do a code review (plus the Rule of Three). 'When you need to fix a bug' is one. B is a build issue, not a Fowler trigger. C and D are BetterCode thresholds, not Fowler's 'when' triggers. (Refactoring1 p.5)

questions.stem: Fowler's 'Refactor As You Do a Code Review' trigger is described in the course as territory for which phase?
questions.explanation: A code review surfaces smells that are fixed after the change has been made — Postfactoring territory. Prefactoring is the before-cleanup tied to 'add function'. Concept Location and Impact Analysis are earlier, non-refactoring phases. (Refactoring1 p.5)

questions.stem: Under the Rule of Three, what should you do the SECOND time you encounter similar code?

options.text: Wince and duplicate it anyway, deferring the refactor
Refactor immediately to remove the duplication

questions.explanation: The Rule of Three says: first time do it, second time wince but duplicate, third time refactor. So on the second occurrence you duplicate (with a wince) rather than refactoring. D, A, B all refactor too early per the rule. (Refactoring1 p.5)

questions.stem: What is Prefactoring?

options.text: Refactoring done after Actualization to remove introduced smells
Writing the tests before writing the production code
Adding the new functionality before cleaning the code
Refactoring done before Actualization to make a planned change easy

questions.explanation: Prefactoring is refactoring before Actualization so injecting the new behaviour becomes easy. A is Postfactoring. B is test-first development (TDD), a different idea. C describes Actualization, which adds behaviour. (course; Refactoring1 p.5)

questions.stem: What is Postfactoring?

options.text: Refactoring done after Actualization to remove smells introduced by the change
Refactoring done before Actualization to make a change easy
Reverting a change that broke the build
Running the regression test suite after a release

questions.explanation: Postfactoring is the after-cleanup that removes smells (duplication, long methods) introduced by the new code. B is Prefactoring. C is a revert, not refactoring. D is testing, not refactoring. (course; BetterCode p.57)

questions.stem: What single property distinguishes Prefactoring from Postfactoring?

options.text: Which refactoring catalog they may draw from
Their position relative to the functional change (Actualization)

questions.explanation: Both are behaviour-preserving and use the same catalog and metrics; the only difference is position relative to Actualization (before vs after). C is false — tests support both. D is false — both preserve behaviour. A is false — the same Fowler/Kerievsky catalog applies to both. (course)

questions.stem: Which phase, unlike Prefactoring and Postfactoring, actually DOES change observable behaviour?
questions.explanation: Actualization is where new functionality is injected, so it changes observable behaviour. Prefactoring and Postfactoring are behaviour-preserving. Concept Location, Impact Analysis, and Initiation are analysis/planning phases that change no code behaviour. (course)

questions.stem: In change-process terms, Prefactoring maps to which Fowler trigger, and Postfactoring to which guideline?

options.text: Prefactoring -> Rule of Three; Postfactoring -> Guideline 1 (15 LOC)
Prefactoring -> Guideline 9 (Automated Tests); Postfactoring -> 'Refactor to fix a bug'
Prefactoring -> Guideline 4 (4 params); Postfactoring -> Rule of Three
Prefactoring -> 'Refactor When You Add Function'; Postfactoring -> BetterCode Guideline 10 'leave no smells behind'

questions.explanation: Prefactoring corresponds to Fowler's 'Refactor When You Add Function'; Postfactoring corresponds to BetterCode G10 'Write Clean Code / leave no smells behind'. The other pairings mismatch the mapping the course draws. (course; Refactoring1 p.5; BetterCode p.57)

questions.stem: Which statement about Prefactoring is correct?

options.text: No new functionality is added; it is pure behaviour-preserving restructuring
It is the phase where the new feature is implemented
It always introduces a design pattern
It is performed only when fixing bugs

questions.explanation: Prefactoring adds no functionality — it is pure behaviour-preserving restructuring to make a later change easy. B describes Actualization. C overstates (a pattern is not required). D restricts it wrongly to bug-fixing; it is tied to adding function. (course)

questions.stem: Postfactoring is linked to the 'Boy-Scout rule' from Clean Code. What does that rule require?

options.text: Refactor only on the third repetition
Leave the code cleaner than you found it
Always write a test before any code

questions.explanation: The Boy-Scout rule (Martin) is 'leave the code cleaner than you found it', the spirit of Postfactoring — don't leave the codebase worse. C is TDD. C contradicts the Comments smell fix. A is the Rule of Three, a different idea. (course; BetterCode p.57)

questions.stem: What is a code smell ('Symptom of Bad Code')?

options.text: A compiler warning that blocks the build
A surface indication that usually corresponds to a deeper problem and a candidate refactoring
A confirmed runtime defect that crashes the program

questions.explanation: A smell is a surface symptom usually pointing to a deeper problem and a candidate refactoring — not a defect itself. D and B describe actual failures/defects. A is a build error. A smell need not even require action. (Refactoring1 p.6)

questions.stem: Why is it WRONG to say 'a code smell is necessarily a bug'?

options.text: Because every smell is automatically fixed by the compiler
Because smells only appear in test code
Because Fowler lists exactly 10 smells, none of which are bugs
A smell is a symptom that usually corresponds to a deeper problem, but it is not itself a defect and may not require action

questions.explanation: Smells are symptoms, not defects; they may not even require action. A is false — compilers do not fix smells. B is false — smells appear in production code. C miscounts (there are 22 Fowler smells) and the reasoning is wrong. (Refactoring1 p.6)

questions.stem: How many code smells ('Symptoms of Bad Code') does the Fowler deck enumerate?
questions.explanation: The Fowler deck lists 22 code smells across three sets. 10 is the number of SIG/BetterCode guidelines. 7 is the number of Fowler refactoring categories. ~50 is roughly the number of catalog refactorings. (Refactoring1 p.6-8)

questions.stem: Which code smell is 'the same structure appearing in more than one place'?
questions.explanation: Duplicated Code is the same structure in more than one place. Divergent Change is one class changing for many reasons. Feature Envy is a method more interested in another class. Data Clumps are bunches of data that appear together. (Refactoring1 p.6)

questions.stem: Which smell describes 'a method that is too long'?
questions.explanation: Long Method is simply a method that is too long. Large Class is a class doing too much. Long Parameter List is too many parameters. Lazy Class is a class not doing enough to justify itself. (Refactoring1 p.6)

questions.stem: Which smell describes 'a class doing too much, with too many fields and methods'?
questions.explanation: Large Class is a class doing too much (too many fields/methods). Lazy Class is the opposite — too little. Data Class is fields plus getters/setters and nothing else. Middle Man is excessive delegation. (Refactoring1 p.6)

questions.stem: Divergent Change is best described as which of the following?

options.text: One class is commonly changed in different ways for different reasons
Every change forces little edits across many different classes
A method seems more interested in another class than its own
Bunches of data regularly appear together

questions.explanation: Divergent Change = one class changed in many ways for many unrelated reasons (a single-responsibility violation). B is its mirror, Shotgun Surgery. C is Feature Envy. D is Data Clumps. (Refactoring1 p.6)

questions.stem: Shotgun Surgery is best described as which of the following?

options.text: A library class missing methods you need
Every time you make a kind of change you must make many little changes to many different classes
One class is changed in many different ways for many reasons
A class that just forwards calls to another class

questions.explanation: Shotgun Surgery = one kind of change scattered across many classes. C is its mirror, Divergent Change. D is Middle Man. A is Incomplete Library Class. (Refactoring1 p.6)

questions.stem: Which statement correctly distinguishes Divergent Change from Shotgun Surgery?

options.text: Both mean the same thing — one class with many responsibilities
Divergent Change is a refactoring; Shotgun Surgery is a smell
Divergent Change = one class changes for many reasons; Shotgun Surgery = one change touches many classes
Divergent Change = one change touches many classes; Shotgun Surgery = one class changes for many reasons

questions.explanation: They are mirror images: Divergent Change is one class with many reasons to change; Shotgun Surgery is one change rippling across many classes. D swaps them (the classic trap). A wrongly equates them. D miscategorises — both are smells. (Refactoring1 p.6)

questions.stem: Which description matches the Feature Envy smell?

options.text: a class delegates almost everything to another class
two classes do the same thing with different interfaces
an instance variable is set only in certain circumstances
a method seems more interested in a class other than the one it is in

questions.explanation: Feature Envy = a method more interested in another class than its own (it 'envies' that class's data). A is Middle Man. B is Alternative Classes with Different Interfaces. C is Temporary Field. (Refactoring1 p.6)

questions.stem: Which smell describes 'bunches of data that regularly appear together'?
questions.explanation: Data Clumps = groups of data items that keep travelling together (candidates for an object). Data Class is an anaemic class of fields+accessors. Primitive Obsession is overusing primitives. Temporary Field is a field set only sometimes. (Refactoring1 p.6)

questions.stem: Which smell describes 'too many parameters to a method'?
questions.explanation: Long Parameter List = too many parameters to a method. Long Method is about method length, not parameters. Message Chains is a.getB().getC() navigation. Middle Man is excessive delegation. (Refactoring1 p.6)

questions.stem: Which statement defines Primitive Obsession?

options.text: a class that forwards calls to a delegate
repeated checks for a null value
excessive use of primitives, due to reluctance to use small objects for small tasks
a method that returns a value and also changes state

questions.explanation: Primitive Obsession = overusing primitive types instead of small objects. D describes a Separate-Query-from-Modifier situation. A is Middle Man. B is the situation fixed by Introduce Null Object. (Refactoring1 p.7)

questions.stem: The Switch Statements smell points toward replacing type-switching logic with what?
questions.explanation: The Switch Statements smell is type-switching that should become polymorphism (Replace Conditional with Polymorphism). The other options would worsen the design, not fix the smell. (Refactoring1 p.7)

questions.stem: Which description matches the Parallel Inheritance Hierarchies smell?

options.text: every time you make a subclass of one class, you must also make a subclass of another
subclasses do not fulfill the commitments of their superclasses
a class isn't doing enough to justify its maintenance
an instance variable is set only in certain circumstances

questions.explanation: Parallel Inheritance Hierarchies = each new subclass in one hierarchy forces a matching subclass in another. B is Refused Bequest. C is Lazy Class. D is Temporary Field. (Refactoring1 p.7)

questions.stem: Which description matches the Lazy Class smell?

options.text: a class has fields and only getters/setters
a class isn't doing enough to justify its maintenance
a class is doing far too much

questions.explanation: Lazy Class = a class not earning its keep. C is Large Class. D is Middle Man. A is Data Class. (Refactoring1 p.7)

questions.stem: Which description matches the Speculative Generality smell?

options.text: a method is more interested in another class
classes and features are added just because a need for them may arise someday
comments are used to compensate for bad code

questions.explanation: Speculative Generality is over-engineering for hypothetical future needs (a YAGNI violation). D is the Comments smell. A is Data Clumps. B is Feature Envy. (Refactoring1 p.7)

questions.stem: Speculative Generality is the Fowler smell that warns against which tendency?

options.text: Leaving duplicated code in more than one place
Returning a value while also mutating state
Adding hypothetical flexibility for needs that may never arise (over-engineering / over-using patterns)

questions.explanation: Speculative Generality warns against building flexibility for needs that may never come — the Fowler-equivalent of Kerievsky's 'over-using a pattern is a smell'. A is Duplicated Code, B relates to Long Method/G1, C is a query/modifier mix. (Refactoring1 p.7)

questions.stem: Which description matches the Temporary Field smell?

options.text: an instance variable is set only in certain circumstances
a field is public and directly accessed
a method returns a collection directly
a class delegates everything to another

questions.explanation: Temporary Field = an instance variable populated only sometimes, confusing readers. B is fixed by Encapsulate Field. C is fixed by Encapsulate Collection. D is Middle Man. (Refactoring1 p.7)

questions.stem: Message Chains is the smell exemplified by which code shape?

options.text: new Foo(a, b, c, d, e)

questions.explanation: Message Chains = transitive visibility chains like a.getB().getC().getD(). The second is Long Parameter List; the third is a conditional (Decompose/Guard Clauses); the fourth is the Switch Statements smell. (Refactoring1 p.7)

questions.stem: Which description matches the Middle Man smell?

options.text: a long chain of getter calls
excessive delegation — a class that mostly just forwards calls
too little delegation between tightly coupled classes

questions.explanation: Middle Man = excessive delegation; the class adds little beyond forwarding. D describes the opposite tension (Inappropriate Intimacy / Message Chains). A is Message Chains. B is Refused Bequest. (Refactoring1 p.8)

questions.stem: Which description matches the Inappropriate Intimacy smell?

options.text: a class that does nothing but delegate
an array whose elements mean different things
excessive interaction and coupling between classes

questions.explanation: Inappropriate Intimacy = two classes too entangled (excessive coupling/interaction). A is Middle Man. B is a public-field issue. C is fixed by Replace Array with Object. (Refactoring1 p.8)

questions.stem: Which description matches the Alternative Classes with Different Interfaces smell?

options.text: classes do the same thing but have different interfaces for it
a class has only fields and accessors
a library class is missing methods you need

questions.explanation: This smell = classes doing the same thing through differing interfaces (unify them). B is Data Class. C is Incomplete Library Class. D is Middle Man. (Refactoring1 p.8)

questions.stem: Which description matches the Incomplete Library Class smell?

options.text: the library class throws unhandled exceptions
a library class is missing methods you need but you cannot edit it
a library class has too many methods
your own class delegates to a library class

questions.explanation: Incomplete Library Class = a third-party class lacks needed methods and you can't change it (fix: Introduce Foreign Method / Local Extension). C, D, A mischaracterise the smell. (Refactoring1 p.8)

questions.stem: Which description matches the Data Class smell?

options.text: forward all calls to a delegate
set a field only in certain circumstances
have fields, getters and setters, and nothing else (anaemic objects)

questions.explanation: Data Class = fields plus accessors and no behaviour (anaemic). D is Large Class. A is Middle Man. B is Temporary Field. (Refactoring1 p.8)

questions.stem: Which description matches the Refused Bequest smell?

options.text: a superclass and subclass are nearly identical
two sibling classes share duplicated code
a class envies another class's data
subclasses do not fulfill the commitments of their superclasses

questions.explanation: Refused Bequest = subclasses inherit but ignore/override most of the parent (wrong inheritance). A is the Collapse Hierarchy situation. B is Duplicated Code across siblings. C is Feature Envy. (Refactoring1 p.8)

questions.stem: Why is 'delete all comments' the WRONG response to the Comments smell?

options.text: The smell is comments compensating for bad code; the fix is usually Extract Method plus a good name so the comment becomes unnecessary
Comments cannot be deleted once committed to version control
Comments are required by BetterCode Guideline 2

questions.explanation: The Comments smell is comments masking unclear code; the cure is to clarify the code (Extract Method + a self-documenting name), not blanket deletion. B is false. C invents a rule (G2 is about complexity). D is nonsense — comments don't affect branch points. (Refactoring1 p.8)

questions.stem: What does the Comments smell specifically refer to?

options.text: comments used to compensate for bad code
any comment longer than one line
comments written in a foreign language

questions.explanation: The Comments smell is narrowly about comments papering over unclear code — not all comments. The other options describe comments that are not the smell. (Refactoring1 p.8)

questions.stem: How many categories does Fowler organise the refactoring catalog into?
questions.explanation: Fowler groups refactorings into 7 categories. 10 is the SIG guideline count, 4 is the number of SIG levels, 22 is the number of code smells. (Refactoring1 p.9)

questions.stem: Which Fowler category is described as 'packaging code properly'?
questions.explanation: Composing Methods = packaging code properly (Extract/Inline Method, Method Object). Organizing Data = making data easier to work with. Moving Features = reassigning responsibilities. Big Refactorings = large-scale changes. (Refactoring1 p.9)

questions.stem: Which Fowler category is described as 'reassigning responsibilities'?
questions.explanation: Moving Features Between Objects = reassigning responsibilities (Move Method/Field, Extract Class). Composing Methods packages code. Simplifying Conditionals tames if-logic. Making Method Calls Simpler eases interfaces. (Refactoring1 p.9)

questions.stem: Which Fowler category is described as 'making data easier to work with'?
questions.explanation: Organizing Data = making data easier to work with. Composing Methods packages code. Dealing with Generalization moves features around an inheritance hierarchy. Big Refactorings are large-scale changes. (Refactoring1 p.9)

questions.stem: Which Fowler category is described as 'making conditional logic less error-prone'?
questions.explanation: Simplifying Conditional Expressions = making conditional logic less error-prone (Decompose Conditional, Guard Clauses, Replace Conditional with Polymorphism). The others address interfaces, data, and responsibilities respectively. (Refactoring1 p.9)

questions.stem: Which Fowler category is described as 'making interfaces easy to understand and use'?
questions.explanation: Making Method Calls Simpler = making interfaces easy to understand and use (Introduce Parameter Object, Separate Query from Modifier). The others target conditionals, code packaging, and inheritance. (Refactoring1 p.9)

questions.stem: Which Fowler category is described as 'moving features around a hierarchy of inheritance'?
questions.explanation: Dealing with Generalization = moving features around an inheritance hierarchy (Pull Up, Extract Super/Subclass, Form Template Method). Moving Features Between Objects moves between collaborating objects, not up/down a hierarchy. (Refactoring1 p.9)

questions.stem: Which Fowler category is described as 'large-scale changes to code'?
questions.explanation: Big Refactorings = large-scale, multi-step changes (Tease Apart Inheritance, Separate Domain from Presentation). The other three are smaller, more local families. (Refactoring1 p.9)

questions.stem: Which of these is NOT one of Fowler's seven refactoring categories?
questions.explanation: The seven categories are Composing Methods, Moving Features, Organizing Data, Simplifying Conditionals, Making Method Calls Simpler, Dealing with Generalization, Big Refactorings. 'Replacing Conditional Dispatchers' is not a category (it echoes a Kerievsky refactoring name). The others are genuine categories. (Refactoring1 p.9)

questions.stem: Which refactoring is the canonical fix for a Long Method?
questions.explanation: Extract Method turns a fragment into a named method — the standard fix for Long Method (also for Duplicated Code and Comments). Inline Method is its inverse. Move Field and Collapse Hierarchy target data placement and inheritance, not method length. (Refactoring1 p.11)

questions.stem: Which refactoring is the standard fix for Feature Envy?
questions.explanation: Feature Envy (a method more interested in another class) is fixed by Move Method — move it to the class it envies. Extract Class splits a Large Class. Inline Class fixes Lazy Class. Encapsulate Field hides a public field. (Refactoring1 p.15)

questions.stem: Which refactoring is the standard fix for a Large Class?
questions.explanation: Extract Class splits a class doing two jobs into two — the fix for Large Class. Inline Class fixes Lazy Class (the inverse). Move Field relocates one field. Hide Delegate fixes Message Chains. (Refactoring1 p.17)

questions.stem: Which refactoring is the standard fix for a Lazy Class?
questions.explanation: Inline Class folds a class that isn't pulling its weight into another and deletes it — the fix for Lazy Class. Extract Class is the inverse (for Large Class). Extract Superclass removes sibling duplication. Decompose Conditional tames if-logic. (Refactoring1 p.18)

questions.stem: Which refactoring fixes Message Chains by adding methods on the server to hide the delegate?
questions.explanation: Hide Delegate hides a delegate behind server methods, breaking a Message Chain. Remove Middle Man is its inverse (for the Middle Man smell). Move Method and Inline Method address other smells. (Refactoring1 p.19)

questions.stem: Which refactoring fixes the Middle Man smell?
questions.explanation: Remove Middle Man gets the client to call the delegate directly, fixing excessive delegation (Middle Man). Hide Delegate is the inverse. Extract Class and Introduce Foreign Method address other smells. (Refactoring1 p.20)

questions.stem: Hide Delegate and Remove Middle Man are inverse refactorings. How do you choose between them?

options.text: Choose based on which has the lower [Fowler99] page number
By which smell dominates — too much delegation (Middle Man) -> Remove Middle Man; chains/coupling -> Hide Delegate
Always prefer Hide Delegate because hiding is safer
Always prefer Remove Middle Man because fewer methods is simpler

questions.explanation: These inverses pull opposite ways; pick by the dominant smell — Middle Man (over-delegation) calls for Remove Middle Man, Message Chains/coupling call for Hide Delegate. C and D are false absolutes. A is the page-number trap (book pages are not selection criteria). (Refactoring1 p.19-20)

questions.stem: Which refactoring fixes Incomplete Library Class when you need a SINGLE extra method on an unmodifiable class?
questions.explanation: Introduce Foreign Method adds a method in the client class taking the server instance as its first argument — for one missing method. Introduce Local Extension is for several missing methods (subclass/wrapper). The others are unrelated. (Refactoring1 p.21)

questions.stem: Which refactoring fixes Incomplete Library Class when SEVERAL extra methods are needed?
questions.explanation: Introduce Local Extension creates a subclass or wrapper of the library class containing the several extra methods. Introduce Foreign Method handles only a single missing method. The others are unrelated. (Refactoring1 p.22)

questions.stem: Which refactoring fixes Primitive Obsession by turning a data item into an object?
questions.explanation: Replace Data Value with Object promotes a primitive that needs extra data/behaviour into an object — the fix for Primitive Obsession. Replace Array with Object targets arrays. The Encapsulate refactorings hide fields, a different concern. (Refactoring1 p.25)

questions.stem: Which refactoring is the canonical fix for the Switch Statements smell?
questions.explanation: Replace Conditional with Polymorphism moves each leg of a type-switch into an overriding subclass method — the canonical fix for Switch Statements (and BetterCode G2's recommended refactoring). Consolidate/Decompose tidy conditionals but don't remove the type-switch; Introduce Null Object handles null checks. (Refactoring1 p.35)

questions.stem: Which refactoring fixes Data Clumps and Long Parameter List by grouping parameters?
questions.explanation: Introduce Parameter Object replaces a group of parameters that naturally go together with an object — fixing Data Clumps and Long Parameter List (and it is the named fix for BetterCode G4). The others shrink parameters differently or separate queries. (Refactoring1 p.40)

questions.stem: Which refactoring fixes Parallel Inheritance Hierarchies?
questions.explanation: Tease Apart Inheritance splits a hierarchy doing two jobs into two hierarchies linked by delegation — fixing Parallel Inheritance Hierarchies. Extract Hierarchy creates subclasses for special cases. Collapse Hierarchy merges near-identical classes. Form Template Method removes step duplication. (Refactoring1 p.52)

questions.stem: Which refactoring fixes Refused Bequest by replacing inheritance?
questions.explanation: Refused Bequest (subclass ignoring the parent contract) is fixed by Replace Inheritance with Delegation — hold the former superclass in a field and delegate. Replace Delegation with Inheritance is the inverse. The others are unrelated. (Refactoring1 p.49)

questions.stem: Which refactoring fixes Duplicated Code across two sibling classes?
questions.explanation: Extract Superclass pulls common features of two similar classes up into a new superclass — fixing sibling duplication. Extract Subclass splits off instance-specific features. Collapse Hierarchy merges near-identical levels. Inline Class fixes Lazy Class. (Refactoring1 p.46)

questions.stem: Which refactoring fixes Temporary Field by creating a subclass for the conditionally-used features?
questions.explanation: Extract Subclass creates a subclass for features used only in some instances — a fix for Temporary Field (and Large Class). Extract Superclass and Extract Interface go the other direction; Collapse Hierarchy merges levels. (Refactoring1 p.47)

questions.stem: In Extract Method, what do you do with a code fragment that can be grouped together?

options.text: inline it into all of its callers
convert it into a parameter object
turn it into a method whose name explains its purpose

questions.explanation: Extract Method turns a coherent fragment into a named method explaining its purpose. A is Inline Method (the inverse). B is Move Method. C is Introduce Parameter Object. (Refactoring1 p.11)

questions.stem: What is the inverse of Extract Method?
questions.explanation: Inline Method puts a method's body back into its callers and removes it — the inverse of Extract Method. Move Method relocates a method, Replace Method with Method Object handles unextractable long methods, and Extract Class splits a class. (Refactoring1 p.12)

questions.stem: When you have a long method using local variables in a way that blocks Extract Method, which refactoring applies?
questions.explanation: Replace Method with Method Object turns the method into an object so locals become fields, enabling further decomposition — used precisely when tangled locals block Extract Method. The others do not solve the local-variable obstacle. (Refactoring1 p.13)

questions.stem: What is the inverse of Extract Class?
questions.explanation: Inline Class folds a class into another and deletes it — the inverse of Extract Class. Inline Method inverts Extract Method. Collapse Hierarchy merges inheritance levels. Remove Middle Man inverts Hide Delegate. (Refactoring1 p.18)

questions.stem: Replace Inheritance with Delegation and Replace Delegation with Inheritance are inverses. When do you use Replace DELEGATION with Inheritance?

options.text: When a subclass uses only part of the superclass interface
When a class has only fields and accessors
When a method returns a collection directly
When you keep writing many simple delegations for the entire interface of the delegate

questions.explanation: Replace Delegation with Inheritance applies when you are writing many simple delegations covering the whole delegate interface — make the delegator a subclass. A is the trigger for the inverse (Replace Inheritance with Delegation). B and C are unrelated smells. (Refactoring1 p.50)

questions.stem: When is Self Encapsulate Field applied?

options.text: the coupling to a directly-accessed field becomes awkward, so you add getters/setters and use only those
a field is public and must be made private with accessors
a field is used more by another class than its own
a method returns a collection that must be protected

questions.explanation: Self Encapsulate Field adds accessors for a field you currently access directly, when that direct coupling becomes awkward. B is Encapsulate Field (public field). C is Move Field. D is Encapsulate Collection. (Refactoring1 p.24)

questions.stem: What does Encapsulate Collection make a method that returns a collection do?

options.text: split the collection across two classes
return a read-only view and provide add/remove methods
return the live mutable collection for performance
convert the collection into an array

questions.explanation: Encapsulate Collection returns a read-only view and exposes add/remove methods, protecting the internal collection from external mutation. C is the unsafe original. D and A mischaracterise the refactoring. (Refactoring1 p.29)

questions.stem: When is Replace Subclass with Fields used?

options.text: two classes share a common interface subset
a subclass refuses its superclass contract
subclasses vary only in methods that return constant data, so you turn those into superclass fields and delete the subclasses
a class is doing too much work via many conditionals

questions.explanation: Replace Subclass with Fields removes trivial subclasses whose only difference is constant return values, converting them to fields. D is Extract Hierarchy. A is Extract Interface. B is Refused Bequest. (Refactoring1 p.30)

questions.stem: What does Decompose Conditional apply to?

options.text: a sequence of conditionals with the same result
nested conditionals hiding the normal execution path
a type-switch that should be polymorphism
a complicated if-then-else, by extracting methods from the condition, then-part, and else-part

questions.explanation: Decompose Conditional extracts methods from the condition and the then/else parts of a complex conditional. A is Consolidate Conditional Expression. B is Replace Nested Conditional with Guard Clauses. C is Replace Conditional with Polymorphism. (Refactoring1 p.32)

questions.stem: When is Consolidate Conditional Expression applied?

options.text: you have a sequence of conditional tests with the same result, which you combine into one and extract
a method changes state and returns a value
a complex conditional needs its parts extracted
repeated null checks clutter the code

questions.explanation: Consolidate Conditional Expression combines several tests that yield the same result into a single extracted conditional. B is Separate Query from Modifier. C is Decompose Conditional. D is Introduce Null Object. (Refactoring1 p.33)

questions.stem: When is Replace Nested Conditional with Guard Clauses used?

options.text: two classes have different interfaces for the same task
conditional behaviour obscures the normal path, so you use guard clauses for the special cases

questions.explanation: Replace Nested Conditional with Guard Clauses clarifies the normal path by returning early on special cases. C -> Introduce Null Object, D -> Introduce Parameter Object, A -> unify interfaces. (Refactoring1 p.34)

questions.stem: When is Introduce Null Object applied?

options.text: you have an array with positional meanings
you have a long parameter list
you have repeated checks for a null value, replaced by a null object
you have a type-switch over object kinds

questions.explanation: Introduce Null Object replaces repeated null checks with a null object exhibiting do-nothing behaviour. D is Replace Conditional with Polymorphism. A is Replace Array with Object. B is Introduce Parameter Object. (Refactoring1 p.36)

questions.stem: When is Separate Query from Modifier applied to a method?

options.text: is just as clear as its name
is more interested in another class
returns a value AND changes state — split it into a query method and a modifier method

questions.explanation: Separate Query from Modifier enforces Command-Query Separation by splitting a method that both returns a value and mutates state. A -> Introduce Parameter Object, B -> Inline Method, C -> Move Method. (Refactoring1 p.38)

questions.stem: When is Parameterize Method applied?

options.text: several methods do similar things with different hard-coded values, so you add a parameter
a method passes a computed result the receiver could compute itself
a group of parameters naturally go together
a method returns a collection directly

questions.explanation: Parameterize Method merges near-identical methods that differ only by embedded values into one method with a parameter — a flavour of fixing Duplicated Code. B is Replace Parameter with Method. C is Introduce Parameter Object. D is Encapsulate Collection. (Refactoring1 p.39)

questions.stem: How does Replace Parameter with Method shrink a parameter list?

options.text: turning the method into a method object
removing a parameter the receiver can compute itself by invoking the method directly
bundling parameters that travel together into an object
adding a parameter for varying values

questions.explanation: Replace Parameter with Method removes a parameter whose value the receiver can obtain itself — addressing Long Parameter List. C is Introduce Parameter Object. D is Parameterize Method. A is Replace Method with Method Object. (Refactoring1 p.41)

questions.stem: Form Template Method is used when two subclass methods perform similar steps in the same order but the steps differ. What does it produce?

options.text: A Template Method pattern: identical step signatures so the originals match and can be pulled up
A Strategy pattern with interchangeable algorithm objects

questions.explanation: Form Template Method aligns step signatures so the surrounding methods become identical and can be pulled up — implementing the Template Method pattern (Kerievsky lists it as a fix for Duplicated Code). The other patterns are produced by different refactorings. (Refactoring1 p.48)

questions.stem: When is Collapse Hierarchy applied?

options.text: two classes share similar features to pull up
a class is doing work that should be two classes
a subclass refuses its parent's contract
a superclass and subclass are not very different, so you merge them

questions.explanation: Collapse Hierarchy merges a superclass and subclass that are nearly the same (a Lazy-Class-in-a-hierarchy fix). A is Extract Superclass. B is Extract Class. C is Refused Bequest. (Refactoring1 p.43)

questions.stem: When is Extract Interface applied?

options.text: several clients use the same subset of a class's interface, or two classes share part of their interface
a class isn't doing enough to justify itself
constructors on subclasses are nearly identical
a field is used more by another class

questions.explanation: Extract Interface pulls a shared subset of an interface into a named interface. B is Inline Class. C is Pull Up Constructor Body. D is Move Field. (Refactoring1 p.48)

questions.stem: When is Pull Up Constructor Body applied?

options.text: a class delegates everything to another
subclass constructors have mostly identical bodies, so you create a superclass constructor and call it
two methods share steps in the same order with differing details
a method has too many parameters

questions.explanation: Pull Up Constructor Body hoists shared constructor logic into a superclass constructor invoked from the subclasses. C is Form Template Method. D is a parameter smell. A is Middle Man. (Refactoring1 p.46)

questions.stem: The numbers like 'Extract Method (110)' on the slides refer to what?

options.text: The cyclomatic complexity of the example
The number of lines the refactoring removes
The page number in the source book [Fowler99], not the lecture slide number
The lecture slide on which the refactoring appears

questions.explanation: Those parenthesised numbers are [Fowler99]/[Ker05] book page numbers as printed on the slides — a classic exam trap. They are NOT slide numbers (D), not complexity (A), not line counts (B). (Refactoring1 p.11)

questions.stem: How many maintainability guidelines does 'Building Maintainable Software' (BetterCode/SIG) define?
questions.explanation: BetterCode/SIG defines 10 guidelines, operationalised by bettercodehub.com. 7 is Fowler's category count, 22 the smell count, 4 the number of SIG levels. (BetterCode p.3)

questions.stem: Which website operationalises the 10 SIG maintainability guidelines?
questions.explanation: The deck names bettercodehub.com as the tool implementing the 10 guidelines as measurable metrics. The other sites are real but not the one named in the deck. (BetterCode p.3)

questions.stem: What are the four SIG levels at which the guidelines operate?
questions.explanation: The four SIG levels are Unit, Module, Component, System. C and D use related-but-wrong names (the deck's mapping is Unit=method, Module=file/class, Component=package/dir, System=codebase). A is a testing-level list, not the SIG hierarchy. (BetterCode p.9)

questions.stem: At the SIG 'Unit' level, a unit is defined as which of the following?

options.text: A top-level directory, package, or namespace
The whole codebase maintained by a team
The smallest independently executable/testable block: method, procedure, function, or subroutine

questions.explanation: A Unit is the smallest independently executable block (method/procedure/function/subroutine). D is the Module level, C the Component level, D the System level. (BetterCode p.9)

questions.stem: At the SIG 'Module' level, a module is defined as which of the following?
questions.explanation: A Module is a file/class/script. A method/function is the Unit level. A package/namespace is the Component level. The whole system is the System level. (BetterCode p.9)

questions.stem: At the SIG 'Component' level, a component is defined as which of the following?

options.text: A top-level directory, package, namespace, Maven sub-module, or Visual Studio solution

questions.explanation: A Component is a top-level directory/package/namespace/Maven sub-module/VS solution. The other options are smaller granularities (Unit or Module level). (BetterCode p.9)

questions.stem: BetterCode Guideline 1 says: limit the length of code units to how many lines of code?
questions.explanation: G1 'Write Short Units of Code' caps a unit at 15 lines of code. The other numbers are plausible but wrong; the threshold is exactly 15 LOC. (BetterCode p.7)

questions.stem: What is the name of BetterCode Guideline 1?
questions.explanation: G1 is 'Write Short Units of Code' (the 15-LOC rule). G2 is Write Simple Units, G3 is Write Code Once, G4 is Keep Unit Interfaces Small. (BetterCode p.7)

questions.stem: Per BetterCode's measure, what counts as a 'line of code' when measuring unit length for G1?

options.text: Every physical line including blanks and comments
Only lines containing a method call
Only lines containing a branch point
Every line that is non-empty and does not contain only comments

questions.explanation: G1's measure counts every non-empty, non-comment-only line as a line of code. B over-counts (blanks/comments excluded). B and C are arbitrary subsets, not the LOC definition. (BetterCode p.10)

questions.stem: Which refactorings does BetterCode recommend for Guideline 1 (Short Units)?

options.text: Extract Method and Replace Method with Method Object
Replace Conditional with Polymorphism and Introduce Parameter Object
Introduce Null Object and Hide Delegate

questions.explanation: G1 is supported by Extract Method and Replace Method with Method Object (to split long units). B mixes G2's and G4's refactorings. C and D address other guidelines. (BetterCode p.11)

questions.stem: A unit measured for G1 has exactly 15 lines of code. Per the threshold, is it compliant?

options.text: Yes — but only if it also has 0 branch points
Yes — the limit is 15 LOC, so 15 is at (not over) the threshold
No — anything 15 or above violates the rule

questions.explanation: G1 limits units to 15 LOC; a 15-LOC unit sits exactly at the threshold and is compliant (the worked clearAllMatches example is 15 LOC and passes). C mis-states the boundary, D uses the wrong number, A adds an unrelated condition. (BetterCode p.10)

questions.stem: BetterCode Guideline 2 limits the number of branch points per unit to at most how many?
questions.explanation: G2 'Write Simple Units of Code' caps branch points at 4 per unit (which is cyclomatic complexity <= 5, since CC = branch points + 1). 5 confuses the CC value with the branch-point limit; 6 and 10 are other thresholds. (BetterCode p.13)

questions.stem: Limiting branch points to 4 corresponds to which cyclomatic complexity ceiling?
questions.explanation: Because CC = branch points + 1, a 4-branch-point limit is a cyclomatic complexity of 5. 4 forgets the +1. 6 and 3 are off by more. (BetterCode p.16)

questions.stem: Which constructs count as a branch point when computing cyclomatic complexity in BetterCode?
questions.explanation: BetterCode counts if, case, for, && and || each as one branch point and adds 1. B wrongly includes return/throw. C lists non-branching constructs. D omits for/&&/||. (BetterCode p.16)

questions.stem: A method contains one while loop and one for loop and nothing else conditional. What is its cyclomatic complexity per BetterCode?
questions.explanation: Two branch points (the loops) + 1 = cyclomatic complexity 3 — exactly the worked clearAllMatches figure. C forgets the +1. D and A miscount the branch points. (BetterCode p.16)

questions.stem: A unit has 4 branch points. Per Guideline 2, does it pass, and what is its cyclomatic complexity?
questions.explanation: G2 allows up to 4 branch points, so 4 passes; CC = 4 + 1 = 5. D wrongly fails it. A forgets the +1. D miscalculates CC. The deck's isMultiPartForm has 4 branch points and still passes. (BetterCode p.17)

questions.stem: Which refactoring does BetterCode recommend for Guideline 2 (Simple Units)?
questions.explanation: G2's recommended refactoring is Replace Conditional with Polymorphism (to remove type-switching complexity). Introduce Parameter Object is G4's, Extract Method is G1's, Encapsulate Collection is unrelated. (BetterCode p.18)

questions.stem: BetterCode states simple units are easier to do what (motivation for G2)?
questions.explanation: G2's motivation: simple units are easier to modify and test. The other pairs are not the stated motivation. (BetterCode p.14)

questions.stem: What is the rule of BetterCode Guideline 3?
questions.explanation: G3 'Write Code Once' = do not copy code; write reusable code or call existing methods. The others are G1, G4, and G9 respectively. (BetterCode p.20)

questions.stem: Per BetterCode Guideline 3, a duplicated block counts as a violation when it is at least how many lines of code?
questions.explanation: G3 flags duplicated blocks of >= 6 lines of code ('WRONG WAY GO BACK'). 4 and 15 are other thresholds (params, unit length); 10 is the duplicated-line count in the worked example, not the threshold. (BetterCode p.23)

questions.stem: Why does BetterCode warn against duplicated code (G3 motivation)?

options.text: Duplicated code increases runtime memory usage
Duplicated code always increases cyclomatic complexity
When code is copied, bugs must be fixed in multiple places, making it harder to analyze and modify

questions.explanation: G3's motivation is that copied code means bugs must be fixed in many places, hurting analysis/modification. A is about runtime, not the stated reason. B and C conflate G3 with G2/G4. (BetterCode p.21)

questions.stem: In the worked G3 example, getVariableToTypeMapping has 15 LOC, 2 parameters, 1 branch point, and 10 lines duplicated with another method. Why does it violate G3?

options.text: The duplicated block (10 lines) exceeds the 6-line duplication threshold

questions.explanation: It violates G3 because the 10-line duplicated block is over the 6-line duplication threshold. It is exactly at (not over) the 15-LOC limit, well under 4 parameters, and CC = 1+1 = 2 — so B, C, D are all fine. (BetterCode p.23)

questions.stem: BetterCode Guideline 4 limits the number of parameters per unit to at most how many?
questions.explanation: G4 'Keep Unit Interfaces Small' caps parameters at 4. The other numbers are plausible distractors but the threshold is 4. (BetterCode p.25)

questions.stem: According to BetterCode, how do you primarily achieve Guideline 4 (small interfaces)?
questions.explanation: G4 is met by extracting parameters into objects (Introduce Parameter Object). Removing branch points is G2, deleting comments relates to G10/Comments smell, automating tests is G9. (BetterCode p.25)

questions.stem: Which Fowler refactoring is the named fix for BetterCode Guideline 4?
questions.explanation: Introduce Parameter Object is the named fix for G4 (group related parameters into an object). Replace Conditional with Polymorphism is G2's, Extract Method is G1's, Form Template Method addresses duplication. (BetterCode p.25)

questions.stem: A unit header lists exactly 4 parameters. Per Guideline 4, is it compliant?

options.text: Yes — the limit is at most 4 parameters
No — anything 4 or more violates G4
Only if it also has fewer than 4 branch points

questions.explanation: G4 allows up to 4 parameters, so 4 is compliant. B mis-states the boundary, C uses the wrong number, D adds an unrelated G2 condition. (BetterCode p.28)

questions.stem: What does BetterCode Guideline 5 require?

options.text: Separate Concerns in Modules to achieve loose coupling between them

questions.explanation: G5 'Separate Concerns in Modules' avoids large modules to achieve loose coupling, hiding implementation behind interfaces. The others are G1, G7, and G9. (BetterCode p.31)

questions.stem: At which SIG level does Guideline 5 (Separate Concerns) operate?
questions.explanation: G5 operates at the Module level. G1-G4 and G10 are Unit-level, G6/G7 are Component-level, G8/G9 are System-level. (BetterCode p.5)

questions.stem: How does BetterCode measure module coupling for Guideline 5?

options.text: Cyclomatic complexity summed over the module
The number of parameters across the module
The percentage of hidden code in the component
Fan-in at the file level — dependencies from units outside the module into units inside it

questions.explanation: G5 measures fan-in: dependencies from outside the module into it, plus module size. A is G2's metric, B is G4's, C is G6's hidden-code metric. (BetterCode p.34)

questions.stem: On BetterCode's fan-in coupling table, how is a module with fan-in in the 1-10 band rated?
questions.explanation: The fan-in bands are 1-10 Excellent, 11-20 Good, 21-50 Difficult, >50 Impossible. 1-10 is Excellent. The other labels apply to higher bands. (BetterCode p.34)

questions.stem: On BetterCode's fan-in coupling table, a module with fan-in of 30 falls into which band and rating?
questions.explanation: Fan-in of 30 sits in the 21-50 band, rated Difficult. 11-20 is Good (too low for 30), 1-10 Excellent, >50 Impossible (too high for 30). (BetterCode p.34)

questions.stem: On the fan-in table, which band is rated 'Impossible' for modifiability/testability?
questions.explanation: Fan-in greater than 50 is rated Impossible. 21-50 is Difficult, 11-20 Good, 1-10 Excellent. (BetterCode p.34)

questions.stem: What does BetterCode Guideline 6 require?
questions.explanation: G6 'Couple Architecture Components Loosely' minimises the code exposed to other components. G7 is balance, G8 is codebase size, G10 is clean code. (BetterCode p.36)

questions.stem: Which SIG level do Guidelines 6 and 7 operate at?
questions.explanation: G6 (loose coupling) and G7 (balanced components) are Component-level guidelines. G5 is Module, G1-G4/G10 are Unit, G8/G9 are System. (BetterCode p.5)

questions.stem: What does BetterCode's 'hidden code' metric for Guideline 6 measure?

options.text: The weighted percentage of code in modules with no external calls (or only outgoing calls), versus interface modules
The number of lines longer than 15
The count of magic constants in a component

questions.explanation: G6 computes the weighted % of hidden code (modules not callable from other components) vs interface modules — the higher the % hidden, the more independent the component. C/D are unrelated, A is G5's fan-in. (BetterCode p.39)

questions.stem: In BetterCode's G6 terminology, a module that has incoming AND outgoing calls across the component boundary is called what?
questions.explanation: A module with both incoming and outgoing external calls is an interface module of the 'throughput' kind; hidden modules have no external calls (or only outgoing). The other terms are not used this way. (BetterCode p.39)

questions.stem: BetterCode Guideline 7 says the number of top-level components should be close to what?
questions.explanation: G7 'Keep Architecture Components Balanced' targets ~9 components (between 6 and 12), of roughly equal size. The other ranges are wrong. (BetterCode p.42)

questions.stem: Beyond the count, what else does Guideline 7 require of top-level components?

options.text: They should be of approximately equal size
They should each be under 15 lines
They should each expose at most 4 parameters
They should each have fan-in under 10

questions.explanation: G7 requires components be both near 9 in number AND of approximately equal size. B/C are Unit-level rules, D is the G5 fan-in band — none is the second half of G7. (BetterCode p.42)

questions.stem: A system has 9 top-level components but one of them is ten times the size of the others. Does it satisfy Guideline 7?

options.text: Yes — size balance only matters for modules, not components
No — G7 also requires components be of approximately equal size
Yes — 9 is the ideal count, so it passes regardless of size

questions.explanation: G7 has two halves: a count near 9 AND approximately equal size. A lopsided component fails the size half even with the ideal count. C ignores the size requirement, D uses a wrong count, A wrongly restricts balance to modules. (BetterCode p.42)

questions.stem: What does BetterCode Guideline 8 require?

options.text: Keep your codebase as small as feasible

questions.explanation: G8 'Keep Your Codebase Small' actively reduces system size. G7 is balance, G6 is coupling, G4 is parameters. (BetterCode p.48)

questions.stem: Which SIG level do Guidelines 8 and 9 operate at?
questions.explanation: G8 (small codebase) and G9 (automated tests) are System-level guidelines. G6/G7 are Component, G5 is Module, G1-G4/G10 are Unit. (BetterCode p.5)

questions.stem: BetterCode's motivation for G8 includes which claim about large systems?

options.text: Large systems have higher defect density and are more likely to fail
Large systems always run faster due to caching
Large systems automatically have loose coupling

questions.explanation: G8's motivation: projects aiming to build a large codebase are more likely to fail, and large systems have higher defect density. B/C/D are false claims not made by the deck. (BetterCode p.49)

questions.stem: BetterCode's G8 size measure expresses system size as man-months. How is it computed?

options.text: Lines of code divided by average developer productivity (LOC per year)
Lines of code multiplied by cyclomatic complexity

questions.explanation: Man-months = LOC / average developer productivity (LOC/year) — the months one person would need to build the system. C/D/A are unrelated formulas. (BetterCode p.51)

questions.stem: What does BetterCode Guideline 9 require?

options.text: Automate tests for your codebase using a test framework

questions.explanation: G9 'Automated Tests' requires automating tests with a framework. G8 is size, G1 is short units, G7 is balance. (BetterCode p.53)

questions.stem: Which is a motivation BetterCode gives for Guideline 9 (Automated Tests)?

options.text: Tests document the code and writing tests makes you write better code

questions.explanation: G9's motivations: testing becomes repeatable, development efficient, code predictable, tests document the code, and writing tests makes you write better code. A/B/C confuse other guidelines' concerns. (BetterCode p.54)

questions.stem: How does Guideline 9 (Automated Tests) connect to refactoring's behaviour-preservation guarantee?

options.text: Tests are the safety net that lets behaviour preservation be verified rather than assumed
Tests reduce the number of branch points automatically
Tests convert duplicated code into reusable methods

questions.explanation: Automated tests verify that observable behaviour is preserved after refactoring — the safety net linking G9 to the Verification phase. B/C/D ascribe unrelated effects to tests. (BetterCode p.54)

questions.stem: What does BetterCode Guideline 10 require?

options.text: Write clean code — leave no code smells behind after development work

questions.explanation: G10 'Write Clean Code' = leave no smells behind after development ('clean code is maintainable code') — the Postfactoring discipline. The others are G9, G7, G2. (BetterCode p.57)

questions.stem: BetterCode Guideline 10 is described as literally embodying which change-process activity?
questions.explanation: G10 'leave no smells behind after development work' is literally the Postfactoring discipline. Prefactoring precedes the change; Actualization adds behaviour; Concept Location is earlier. (BetterCode p.57)

questions.stem: Which is one of the seven 'How To' rules for G10 (Write Clean Code)?
questions.explanation: G10's seven rules include: no unit-level smells, no bad comments, no code in comments, no dead code, no long identifier names, no magic constants, no badly handled exceptions. 'No magic constants' is one. The others are G5/G2/G7 thresholds. (BetterCode p.58)

questions.stem: Which of these is NOT one of G10's seven 'How To' clean-code rules?
questions.explanation: G10's seven rules cover smells, bad comments, code in comments, dead code, long identifiers, magic constants, and badly handled exceptions. The 15-line limit is G1, not a G10 clean-code rule. The other three are genuine G10 rules. (BetterCode p.58)

questions.stem: Which guideline maps to the Module level in the SIG hierarchy?
questions.explanation: Only G5 sits at the Module level. G1 is Unit, G9 is System, G6 is Component. (BetterCode p.5)

questions.stem: Which set lists guidelines that all live at the Unit level?
questions.explanation: Unit-level guidelines are G1 (short), G2 (simple), G3 (write once), G4 (small interfaces), and G10 (clean code). The other sets mix in Module (G5), Component (G6/G7), and System (G8/G9) guidelines. (BetterCode p.5)

questions.stem: Whose book is the source of the high-level / 'Refactoring to Patterns' material?
questions.explanation: The high-level/composite refactoring deck is Kerievsky's 'Refactoring to Patterns' [Ker05]. Fowler wrote the low-level catalog, SIG/Visser wrote 'Building Maintainable Software', and the Gang of Four wrote 'Design Patterns'. (HighLevelRefactoring p.2)

questions.stem: In Kerievsky's metaphor 'Design patterns are the word problems of the programming world; refactoring is its ___', which word fills the blank?
questions.explanation: Kerievsky's metaphor: patterns are the word problems, refactoring is the algebra (you solve a design problem via a sequence of behaviour-preserving moves). The other words do not complete the slide's phrase. (HighLevelRefactoring p.9)

questions.stem: What distinguishes high-level (composite) refactorings from Fowler's low-level ones?

options.text: A composite refactoring removes the need for tests
A composite refactoring is a sequence of low-level refactorings that introduces a design pattern
A composite refactoring changes observable behaviour, unlike low-level ones
A composite refactoring is a single atomic, tool-automated step

questions.explanation: High-level/composite refactorings are sequences of small low-level moves that introduce a design pattern. C is false — both preserve behaviour. D describes a low-level refactoring (atomic/automatable), the opposite. A is never true. (HighLevelRefactoring p.16)

questions.stem: Which is the best example of a HIGH-LEVEL (composite) refactoring rather than a low-level one?
questions.explanation: Replace Conditional Logic with Strategy introduces a design pattern via a sequence of steps — a composite/high-level refactoring. Extract Method, Move Field, and Inline Method are atomic, mechanical, low-level Fowler moves. (HighLevelRefactoring p.16)

questions.stem: Which is one of the three benefits Kerievsky gives for composite refactorings?

options.text: They eliminate the need for version control
They automatically reduce parameter counts to 4
They provide an overall plan for a refactoring sequence

questions.explanation: The three benefits: an overall plan for the sequence, suggesting non-obvious design directions, and insight into implementing patterns. A/B/C are unrelated metric/tooling claims. (HighLevelRefactoring p.16)

questions.stem: Which of these is NOT one of Kerievsky's three benefits of composite refactorings?

options.text: They reduce the codebase to under 9 components
They provide an overall plan for the sequence
They give insight into implementing patterns

questions.explanation: The three benefits are: overall plan, non-obvious directions, pattern-implementation insight. 'Reduce to under 9 components' is a garbled echo of G7, not a benefit of composite refactorings. The other three are genuine. (HighLevelRefactoring p.16)

questions.stem: What does Kerievsky's 'Automation First' heuristic advise?

options.text: automate the build pipeline before merging
prefer tool-automated refactorings ('highways') over manual ones ('dirt roads')
automate the test suite before any refactoring
always start refactoring from the deepest subclass

questions.explanation: Automation First: 'manual refactorings are dirt roads, automated refactorings are highways; look first for the highways.' C is about tests (G9), D/A are unrelated. (HighLevelRefactoring p.20)

questions.stem: What does Kerievsky's 'Client First' heuristic advise?

options.text: ask the client (customer) which refactoring to apply
always refactor the most-used class first
start refactoring from a client of the smelly code to find a simpler automated path
refactor the client-facing API before the internals

questions.explanation: Client First: begin from a client of the smelly code to discover a simpler, automatable path. D/A/B misread 'client' as an API layer or a customer or a usage-frequency rule. (HighLevelRefactoring p.20)

questions.stem: Why does Kerievsky's 'Refactoring Directions' matrix include moves AWAY from patterns (e.g. Inline Singleton)?

options.text: Because patterns must always be removed before release
Because moving away from a pattern reduces line count below 15
Because over-applying a pattern is itself a smell, so sometimes you refactor away from a pattern

questions.explanation: Over-using a pattern is a smell, so the matrix lists moves both toward and away (e.g. Inline Singleton moves away from Singleton). A/B/C are false — patterns are not forbidden or always removed, and this is not about line counts. (HighLevelRefactoring p.22)

questions.stem: In Kerievsky's catalog, which refactoring addresses the Switch Statements smell by introducing a Command?
questions.explanation: Replace Conditional Dispatcher with Command targets Switch Statements (and Long/Large smells) via the Command pattern. Replace Conditional Logic with Strategy uses Strategy; Form Template Method targets duplication; Unify Interfaces with Adapter targets differing interfaces. (HighLevelRefactoring p.13)

questions.stem: In Kerievsky's catalog, Conditional Complexity can be refactored toward which pattern via 'Replace Conditional Logic with Strategy'?
questions.explanation: Replace Conditional Logic with Strategy introduces the Strategy pattern to tame Conditional Complexity. Singleton, Adapter, and Composite are reached by other Kerievsky refactorings. (HighLevelRefactoring p.13)

questions.stem: In Kerievsky's catalog, 'Alternative Classes with Different Interfaces' is unified using which pattern?
questions.explanation: Unify Interfaces with Adapter resolves Alternative Classes with Different Interfaces. Decorator, Strategy, and Visitor target other smells (embellishment, conditional logic, accumulation). (HighLevelRefactoring p.12)

questions.stem: Which Kerievsky-only smell (not in Fowler's list) is fixed by 'Replace Implicit Language with Interpreter'?
questions.explanation: Combinatorial Explosion is a Kerievsky-only smell addressed by Replace Implicit Language with Interpreter. Duplicated Code, Large Class, and Primitive Obsession are Fowler smells (marked [F]) with other Kerievsky fixes. (HighLevelRefactoring p.12)

questions.stem: Which of these smells appears ONLY in Kerievsky's catalog, not in Fowler's 22?
questions.explanation: Kerievsky-only smells include Combinatorial Explosion, Conditional Complexity, Indecent Exposure, Oddball Solution, and Solution Sprawl. Long Method, Large Class, and Switch Statements are all Fowler smells too (marked [F]). (HighLevelRefactoring p.13)

questions.stem: In Kerievsky's catalog, Long Method can be refactored using which composite refactoring?
questions.explanation: Compose Method is Kerievsky's fix for Long Method (alongside Move Accumulation to Collecting Parameter, etc.). Unify Interfaces with Adapter targets differing interfaces, Encapsulate Classes with Factory targets Indecent Exposure, Inline Singleton targets Lazy Class. (HighLevelRefactoring p.13)

questions.stem: In Kerievsky's catalog, the Lazy Class smell is addressed by which refactoring (moving AWAY from a pattern)?
questions.explanation: Inline Singleton (which moves away from the Singleton pattern) addresses Lazy Class in Kerievsky's catalog. The others target Indecent Exposure, Conditional/Primitive smells, and Duplicated Code respectively. (HighLevelRefactoring p.13)

questions.stem: Kerievsky says you can refactor 'towards', 'further into', or 'away from' a pattern. What does this imply about reaching a pattern?

options.text: There is exactly one canonical refactoring path to each pattern
Patterns can only be added, never removed
Only Fowler's low-level moves can reach a pattern
A pattern can be reached from different directions, and over-applying one is itself a smell

questions.explanation: A pattern can be approached many ways, and the matrix even lists moves away because over-application is a smell. B contradicts 'many ways'. C contradicts the 'away' moves. C is false — composite refactorings reach patterns. (HighLevelRefactoring p.21)

questions.stem: The high-level deck's 'Hello World' implemented three ways (Strategy / Factory / Main) makes which point?

options.text: There are many ways to implement a pattern
Hello World should always use Strategy

questions.explanation: Showing the same Hello World as Strategy, Factory, and Main illustrates 'There Are Many Ways To Implement A Pattern.' B/C/D are not the lesson; the deck deliberately shows variety. (HighLevelRefactoring p.18)

questions.stem: The deck uses the Jefferson-to-Franklin revision of the Declaration of Independence ('self-evident' replacing 'sacred and undeniable') as a metaphor for what?

options.text: Removing all design patterns from code
Counting branch points in a unit

questions.explanation: The Declaration revision is a metaphor for iterative, meaning-preserving improvement of expression — the spirit of refactoring. C/D/A are unrelated to the metaphor. (HighLevelRefactoring p.6)

questions.stem: When is the Big Refactoring 'Separate Domain from Presentation' applied?

options.text: procedural code needs to become objects
a class is doing too much through many conditionals
GUI classes contain domain logic, which you move into separate domain classes

questions.explanation: Separate Domain from Presentation pulls domain logic out of GUI classes into domain classes (relevant to MVC/JHotDraw). D is Tease Apart Inheritance, A is Convert Procedural Design to Objects, B is Extract Hierarchy. (Refactoring1 p.56)

questions.stem: The Big Refactoring 'Convert Procedural Design to Objects' does what?

options.text: Splits one inheritance hierarchy into two
Creates a subclass per special case from conditionals
Turns data records into objects and moves the behaviour onto them

questions.explanation: Convert Procedural Design to Objects turns data records into objects and relocates behaviour onto them. A is Tease Apart Inheritance, B is Separate Domain from Presentation, C is Extract Hierarchy. (Refactoring1 p.55)

questions.stem: When is the Big Refactoring 'Extract Hierarchy' applied?

options.text: a class does too much through many conditionals, so you create a subclass per special case

questions.explanation: Extract Hierarchy replaces a conditional-heavy class with a hierarchy where each subclass is a special case — the big-refactoring cousin of Replace Conditional with Polymorphism. B is Separate Domain from Presentation, C is Extract Superclass, D is Extract Method. (Refactoring1 p.57)

questions.stem: Which Big Refactoring is the large-scale cousin of 'Replace Conditional with Polymorphism'?
questions.explanation: Extract Hierarchy turns conditional-driven special cases into a subclass hierarchy — the big-scale analogue of Replace Conditional with Polymorphism. The others address parallel hierarchies, procedural code, and GUI/domain mixing. (Refactoring1 p.57)

questions.stem: What is JHotDraw used as in this course?

options.text: The static analysis tool used in the lab
The version-control system for the course
The running case study and the lab's target codebase
The build server for continuous integration

questions.explanation: JHotDraw is the course's running case study and the lab target. The lab's static analyser is SonarLint (not JHotDraw); JHotDraw is not a build server or VCS. (RefactLab p.1)

questions.stem: Why is Kerievsky's 'refactoring to patterns' deck especially applicable to JHotDraw?

options.text: JHotDraw has no design patterns, so every refactoring adds one
JHotDraw is written procedurally, so only Convert Procedural Design applies
JHotDraw forbids inheritance, so only delegation refactorings apply
JHotDraw is a GoF design-pattern showcase, so its patterns are exactly the targets of Kerievsky's refactorings

questions.explanation: JHotDraw heavily uses GoF patterns (Factory Method, Strategy, Composite, Observer, Decorator, etc.), which are precisely what Kerievsky's refactorings target. A/B/C mischaracterise JHotDraw, which is rich in patterns and inheritance. (course)

questions.stem: Which JHotDraw construct exemplifies the Composite pattern?
questions.explanation: CompositeFigure containing Figures is JHotDraw's Composite. createFigure is Factory Method, DrawingView/Drawing is Observer, an undoable command is Command. (course)

questions.stem: In JHotDraw, the method 'createFigure' is an example of which pattern?
questions.explanation: createFigure is a Factory Method in JHotDraw. Composite is CompositeFigure, Observer is figures notifying views, Strategy is handles/tools. (course)

questions.stem: Which Big Refactoring corresponds to JHotDraw's MVC-style split between the Drawing/Figure domain model and the DrawingView/GUI presentation?
questions.explanation: Separate Domain from Presentation maps onto JHotDraw's domain (Drawing/Figure) vs presentation (DrawingView/GUI) layering. The others address inheritance, conditionals, and procedural code. (Refactoring1 p.56)

questions.stem: Which Fowler refactoring (Organizing Data) supports the GUI/domain synchronisation seen in JHotDraw's MVC design?
questions.explanation: Duplicate Observed Data copies GUI-control data into a domain object with an observer to keep them in sync — exactly the JHotDraw domain/presentation separation. The other Organizing Data refactorings serve different ends. (Refactoring1 p.27)

questions.stem: In a JHotDraw change request 'add a new figure type', which describes the correct Prefactoring -> Actualization -> Postfactoring flow?

options.text: Prefactoring cleans the Figure/Tool code to make the addition easy; Actualization adds the new Figure/Tool; Postfactoring removes any duplication introduced
Actualization cleans the code; Prefactoring adds the figure; Postfactoring writes tests
Prefactoring adds the figure; Actualization removes smells; Postfactoring locates the concept
All three phases add new behaviour to the Figure hierarchy
