questions.explanation: Prefactoring (clean to ease the change) precedes Actualization (add the Figure/Tool), then Postfactoring removes introduced duplication. B/C swap the roles; D is false since only Actualization adds behaviour. (course)

questions.stem: Which tool does the lab use to detect code smells in JHotDraw?
questions.explanation: The lab uses SonarLint inside the IDE to find smells in JHotDraw. BetterCodeHub is the SIG guideline tool (from the lecture, not the lab tool here), Git is version control, JUnit is a test framework. (RefactLab p.1)

questions.stem: On what kind of branch does the lab ask you to apply the refactorings?
questions.explanation: The lab's workflow uses a feature branch for the refactoring work. Working directly on main, a detached HEAD, or a release tag are not the prescribed workflow. (RefactLab p.1)

questions.stem: Which catalog of refactorings does the lab ask you to APPLY to the JHotDraw smells?
questions.explanation: The lab directs you to apply suitable Kerievsky [Ker05] refactoring patterns to remove the smells. A/B/C mischaracterise the lab task. (RefactLab p.1)

questions.stem: Which sequence best describes the lab workflow?

options.text: Create feature branch -> run SonarLint -> identify smells in JHotDraw -> apply Kerievsky refactorings -> document the strategy
Run JUnit -> commit to main -> measure man-months -> release
Compute fan-in -> balance components -> deploy -> review
Write tests -> compute cyclomatic complexity -> merge -> tag

questions.explanation: The lab workflow is: feature branch, SonarLint, find smells in JHotDraw, apply Kerievsky refactorings, document the strategy in the portfolio. The other sequences mix in unrelated metric/release steps. (RefactLab p.1)

questions.stem: In the worked example, clearAllMatches(String input, String word) has 15 LOC, contains a while and a for, and 2 parameters. Against G1, G2, and G4, how does it score?

options.text: G1 at threshold (15 LOC), G2 passes (CC 3), G4 passes (2 params)
G1 fails (over 15), G2 fails (CC 5), G4 passes
G1 passes, G2 fails (CC 6), G4 fails (5 params)

questions.explanation: 15 LOC sits exactly at G1's threshold (compliant); 2 branch points + 1 = CC 3, well under G2's limit; 2 parameters are under G4's 4. So G1 at-threshold, G2 and G4 pass. The other options miscount. (BetterCode p.10,16,28)

questions.stem: In the deck's G2 comparison, doAutoCompleteLabel has 3 branch points and isMultiPartForm has 4. Which pass Guideline 2?

options.text: Both pass — each is at or below the 4-branch-point limit

questions.explanation: G2 allows up to 4 branch points, so 3 and 4 both pass — the deck uses them to show where the line sits. D/A/B wrongly fail one or both. (BetterCode p.17)

questions.stem: After refactoring the G3 duplication example, the second method shrinks to 15 LOC, 1 parameter, 1 branch point by calling functionHeader.copy() and a shared helper. Which refactorings does this illustrate?

options.text: Inline Method plus Remove Middle Man
Replace Conditional with Polymorphism plus Form Template Method
Extract Method plus Introduce Parameter Object, verified by re-measuring

questions.explanation: Factoring the shared logic out (Extract Method) and bundling/reducing parameters (Introduce Parameter Object) removes the duplication, confirmed by re-measuring. The other pairs are unrelated to this example. (BetterCode p.23)

questions.stem: What is the disciplined refactoring loop the lecture teaches?

options.text: measure -> identify smell -> pick refactoring -> apply small behaviour-preserving steps -> re-run tests -> re-measure

questions.explanation: The lecture's loop: measure, identify the smell, pick a catalog refactoring, apply in small behaviour-preserving steps, re-run tests, re-measure — repeat until under threshold. B/C/D are unrelated release/SDLC loops. (BetterCode p.23)

questions.stem: Which statement is the single most common exam trap about refactoring, and is FALSE?

options.text: 'Refactoring changes the observable behaviour of the software'

questions.explanation: The classic trap is the false claim that refactoring changes observable behaviour — by definition it does not. The other three statements are all true of refactoring. (Refactoring1 p.3)

questions.stem: A reviewer says 'this duplicated block is a smell, therefore it is a bug we must fix immediately.' What is wrong with this reasoning?

options.text: Smells can only be detected by SonarLint, so a human cannot claim one
A smell becomes a bug only after 6 duplicated lines
A smell is a symptom, not a defect; it does not always require action and is not necessarily a bug
Duplicated code is never a smell

questions.explanation: Smell != bug: a smell is a symptom that may not require action and is not itself a defect. D is false (duplication IS a smell). A is false (smells are recognisable by definition). B confuses the G3 duplication threshold with bug-hood. (Refactoring1 p.6)

questions.stem: Which pairing of inverse refactorings is INCORRECT?

options.text: Replace Inheritance with Delegation <-> Replace Delegation with Inheritance

questions.explanation: Extract Method's inverse is Inline Method, not Move Method — so this pairing is incorrect. The other three are genuine inverse pairs. (Refactoring1 p.12,18,19)

questions.stem: Middle Man and Message Chains/Inappropriate Intimacy are said to 'pull in opposite directions.' Why?

options.text: Middle Man is too much delegation; Message Chains/Inappropriate Intimacy reflect too little hiding/too much coupling — fixing one can create the other
They are the same smell under two names
Both are fixed by the identical refactoring

questions.explanation: Middle Man = excessive delegation (remove it), while Message Chains/coupling want MORE hiding (Hide Delegate) — over-correcting one drifts toward the other. B/C/D deny the tension that the gotcha highlights. (Refactoring1 p.8,19)

questions.stem: What is the inverse of 'Hide Delegate'?
questions.explanation: Hide Delegate (add server methods to hide a delegate) is inverted by Remove Middle Man (call the delegate directly). The other options invert different refactorings. (Refactoring1 p.20)

questions.stem: Forgetting the '+1' when computing cyclomatic complexity from branch points leads to what error?

options.text: Reporting CC one lower than its true value (e.g. calling a 4-branch-point unit CC 4 instead of 5)
Reporting CC one higher than its true value

questions.explanation: CC = branch points + 1; omitting the +1 understates CC by exactly one (4 branch points is CC 5, not 4). The other options describe unrelated mistakes. (BetterCode p.16)

questions.stem: A teammate refactored a tangled module but ran no tests, saying 'refactoring is behaviour-preserving by definition, so tests are unnecessary.' What is the correct critique?

options.text: Refactoring without tests is fine because the definition guarantees correctness
Tests would change the observable behaviour, so omitting them is correct
Tests are only needed for new features, never for refactoring
Behaviour preservation must be VERIFIED, not assumed — that is why G9 (Automated Tests) and the Verification phase exist

questions.explanation: The definition is an intention; in practice behaviour preservation must be verified by tests (G9 / Verification). B restates the fallacy, B is nonsense (tests don't change behaviour), C is false — refactoring needs the safety net most. (BetterCode p.54)

questions.stem: Which statement about Prefactoring versus Postfactoring is FALSE?

options.text: One of them adds new functionality while the other does not
They differ only by position relative to Actualization
Both may use the same Fowler/Kerievsky catalog

questions.explanation: Neither Prefactoring nor Postfactoring adds functionality — both are behaviour-preserving; the false statement is that one adds functionality. The other three statements are all true. (course)

questions.stem: Over-applying a design pattern is best characterised as which Fowler smell?
questions.explanation: Over-engineering flexibility you may never need — including over-using patterns — is the Speculative Generality smell (the Fowler analogue of Kerievsky's 'refactor away from a pattern'). The other smells describe inheritance misuse, anaemic classes, and conditional fields. (Refactoring1 p.7)

questions.stem: Which row lists the SIG numeric thresholds correctly in the order unit length / branch points / duplication block / parameters?
questions.explanation: The SIG thresholds are 15 LOC (G1), <=4 branch points (G2), duplication >=6 lines (G3), <=4 parameters (G4). The other rows corrupt one or more numbers. (BetterCode p.7,13,23,25)

questions.stem: Which of the following is a Fowler code smell?
questions.explanation: Shotgun Surgery is one of the 22 Fowler smells. Loose Coupling is a design quality (and a SIG goal), Continuous Integration is a process practice, and Pair Programming is a development practice — none is a code smell. (Refactoring1 p.6)

questions.stem: Which of the following is a Fowler code smell rather than a design or process technique?
questions.explanation: Inappropriate Intimacy is one of the 22 Fowler smells (excessive coupling between classes). The others are design/process techniques, not smells. (Refactoring1 p.8)

questions.stem: Which of the following is a Fowler refactoring in the Composing Methods category?
questions.explanation: Extract Method belongs to Composing Methods. Move Field is in Moving Features, Extract Superclass in Dealing with Generalization, Introduce Null Object in Simplifying Conditionals. (Refactoring1 p.11)

questions.stem: Which refactoring is the inverse of Inline Method?
questions.explanation: Extract Method (fragment -> named method) is the inverse of Inline Method (body -> callers). The others invert different refactorings or none. (Refactoring1 p.12)

questions.stem: Which smell is the inverse situation of Divergent Change?
questions.explanation: Shotgun Surgery (one change -> many classes) is the inverse of Divergent Change (one class -> many reasons). Feature Envy, Lazy Class, and Data Class are unrelated smells. (Refactoring1 p.6)

questions.stem: What does BetterCode Guideline 3 ('Write Code Once') tell you NOT to do?
questions.explanation: G3 says do not copy code (write reusable code or call existing methods). Writing tests, using interfaces, and splitting modules are encouraged elsewhere. (BetterCode p.20)

questions.stem: Which BetterCode guideline carries the '15 lines of code' threshold?
questions.explanation: The 15-LOC threshold is G1 (Short Units). G2 is branch points, G3 is duplication, G4 is parameters. (BetterCode p.7)

questions.stem: Which BetterCode guideline carries the '6-line duplication' threshold?
questions.explanation: The >=6-line duplication threshold is G3 (Write Code Once). G1 is unit length, G5 is module coupling, G7 is component balance. (BetterCode p.23)

questions.stem: Which BetterCode guideline carries the 'at most 4 parameters' threshold?
questions.explanation: The <=4 parameters threshold is G4 (Keep Unit Interfaces Small). G2 is branch points, G6 is component coupling, G8 is codebase size. (BetterCode p.25)

questions.stem: Which BetterCode guideline targets cyclomatic complexity via a 'branch points' limit?
questions.explanation: G2 (Simple Units) limits branch points (CC). G1 is length, G4 is parameters, G9 is tests. (BetterCode p.13)

questions.stem: What is the name of BetterCode Guideline 10?
questions.explanation: G10 is 'Write Clean Code' (leave no smells behind). G9 is Automated Tests, G8 is Small Codebase, G5 is Separate Concerns. (BetterCode p.57)

questions.stem: Which refactoring is the standard fix for Duplicated Code that appears in two methods of the same class?
questions.explanation: Extract Method pulls the shared fragment into one named method called by both — the standard fix for Duplicated Code within a class. The others address class size, hierarchies, and field visibility. (Refactoring1 p.11)

questions.stem: Cyclomatic complexity equals the number of branch points plus what?
questions.explanation: CC = branch points + 1. It is not +0, and it has nothing to do with parameter or line counts. (BetterCode p.16)

questions.stem: At which SIG level does Guideline 1 (Short Units) operate?
questions.explanation: G1 is a Unit-level guideline. Module is G5, Component is G6/G7, System is G8/G9. (BetterCode p.5)

questions.stem: At which SIG level does Guideline 9 (Automated Tests) operate?
questions.explanation: G9 (Automated Tests) is a System-level guideline. Unit covers G1-G4/G10, Module is G5, Component is G6/G7. (BetterCode p.55)

questions.stem: Which smell is described as 'a class that just forwards calls to another class'?
questions.explanation: Middle Man is excessive delegation — a class that just forwards. Lazy Class does too little overall, Data Class is fields+accessors, Large Class does too much. (Refactoring1 p.8)

questions.stem: Which smell is the YAGNI ('You Aren't Gonna Need It') violation in Fowler's list?
questions.explanation: Speculative Generality is the YAGNI violation — building for needs that may never arise. The other smells describe conditional fields, grouped data, and getter chains. (Refactoring1 p.7)

questions.stem: Which refactoring turns a public field into a private field with accessors?
questions.explanation: Encapsulate Field makes a public field private and provides accessors. Self Encapsulate Field adds accessors for a field you already access directly; Encapsulate Collection protects collections; Move Field relocates a field. (Refactoring1 p.28)

questions.stem: Which refactoring replaces an array whose elements mean different things with an object having a field per element?
questions.explanation: Replace Array with Object gives each positional array element its own named field on an object. Replace Data Value with Object promotes a single primitive; the others handle collections and subclasses. (Refactoring1 p.26)

questions.stem: Which is one of Fowler's four reasons to refactor?

options.text: Refactoring replaces all comments with code
Refactoring makes software easier to understand
Refactoring increases the number of classes

questions.explanation: 'Makes software easier to understand' is one of the four reasons. The others are not reasons Fowler lists. (Refactoring1 p.4)

questions.stem: Which Fowler refactoring is recommended to remove a type-based switch statement?
questions.explanation: Replace Conditional with Polymorphism is the canonical Switch Statements fix. Consolidate Conditional Expression merges same-result tests, Parameterize Method merges value-varying methods, Hide Delegate breaks chains. (Refactoring1 p.35)

questions.stem: Which deck covers the 10 measurable SIG maintainability guidelines?
questions.explanation: BetterCode.pdf ('Building Maintainable Software') covers the 10 SIG guidelines. Refactoring1.pdf is Fowler's catalog, HighLevelRefactoring.pdf is Kerievsky, RefactoringLab1.pdf is the lab. (BetterCode p.3)

questions.stem: Which deck covers Fowler's low-level refactoring catalog and the 22 code smells?
questions.explanation: Refactoring1.pdf is Fowler's catalog with the 22 smells. BetterCode is SIG, HighLevelRefactoring is Kerievsky, RefactoringLab1 is the lab. (Refactoring1 p.6)

questions.stem: Which deck covers Kerievsky's 'Refactoring to Patterns' (high-level/composite refactoring)?
questions.explanation: HighLevelRefactoring.pdf is Kerievsky's high-level catalog. Refactoring1 is Fowler, BetterCode is SIG, RefactoringLab1 is the lab. (HighLevelRefactoring p.2)

questions.stem: What does a code smell most accurately indicate?

options.text: A possible deeper problem and a candidate refactoring

questions.explanation: A smell indicates a possible deeper problem and a candidate refactoring — a symptom, not a guaranteed failure. The other options describe actual errors/failures. (Refactoring1 p.6)

questions.stem: Which two phases are the 'refactoring bookends' of the change process named in this lecture?
questions.explanation: Prefactoring (before) and Postfactoring (after) bracket Actualization as the refactoring bookends. The other pairs are different phases of Rajlich's process. (course)

questions.stem: In Rajlich's change process, which phase immediately PRECEDES Actualization in this lecture's framing?
questions.explanation: Prefactoring immediately precedes Actualization (clean up so the change is easy). Postfactoring follows it; Concept Location is earlier; Conclusion is later. (course)

questions.stem: In Rajlich's change process, which phase immediately FOLLOWS Actualization in this lecture's framing?
questions.explanation: Postfactoring immediately follows Actualization (remove smells the change introduced). Prefactoring precedes it; Impact Analysis and Initiation are earlier. (course)

questions.stem: Why is Prefactoring valuable BEFORE adding a feature rather than just coding the feature directly?

options.text: It adds the feature faster by skipping tests
It permanently freezes the design so no future change is needed
It removes the need for Impact Analysis
It turns a hard change into an easy one by cleaning the tangled change region first

questions.explanation: Prefactoring restructures the tangled region so injecting the new behaviour becomes easy — turning a hard change into an easy one. A is false (tests stay), B overstates, C is false (Impact Analysis precedes it). (course)

questions.stem: A developer adds a feature, then notices the new code duplicated a method and left a 30-line unit. Cleaning this up is which phase?
questions.explanation: Cleaning up smells introduced by the just-added code is Postfactoring. Prefactoring is before the change; Actualization is the change itself; Concept Location is finding where to change. (course; BetterCode p.57)

questions.stem: Which ordering of the relevant change-process spine is correct for this lecture?

options.text: Actualization -> Prefactoring -> Postfactoring -> Concept Location -> Verification -> Impact Analysis
Concept Location -> Impact Analysis -> Prefactoring -> Actualization -> Postfactoring -> Verification
Impact Analysis -> Concept Location -> Actualization -> Prefactoring -> Verification -> Postfactoring
Prefactoring -> Concept Location -> Actualization -> Postfactoring -> Impact Analysis -> Verification

questions.explanation: The spine runs Concept Location -> Impact Analysis -> Prefactoring -> Actualization -> Postfactoring -> Verification. The other orderings shuffle these phases incorrectly. (course)

questions.stem: Both Prefactoring and Postfactoring draw on which catalogs and metrics?

options.text: Only SIG metrics for Prefactoring and only Fowler for Postfactoring
The same Fowler/Kerievsky catalog and the same SIG metrics
Only Kerievsky for Prefactoring and only Fowler for Postfactoring

questions.explanation: Because they differ only by position, both use the same Fowler/Kerievsky catalog and the same SIG metrics. The other options invent artificial splits. (course)

questions.stem: Given a Long Parameter List where the parameters naturally group together, which refactoring fits best?
questions.explanation: When the parameters naturally go together, Introduce Parameter Object bundles them into one object. Replace Parameter with Method removes a computable parameter (not a natural group); Parameterize Method merges value-varying methods; Separate Query from Modifier is unrelated. (Refactoring1 p.40)

questions.stem: Given a Long Parameter List where one parameter is a value the receiver could compute itself, which refactoring fits best?
questions.explanation: Replace Parameter with Method removes a parameter the receiver can compute itself. Introduce Parameter Object suits a natural group; Parameterize Method merges value-varying methods; Encapsulate Field is unrelated. (Refactoring1 p.41)

questions.stem: Given a method with a deeply nested conditional that hides the normal execution path, which refactoring fits best?

options.text: Replace Nested Conditional with Guard Clauses

questions.explanation: Guard Clauses make the normal path clear by returning early on special cases — the fit for a hidden normal path. Consolidate merges same-result tests; Introduce Null Object handles null checks; Decompose extracts methods from a complex conditional. (Refactoring1 p.34)

questions.stem: Given a complicated if-then-else whose condition and branches are each hard to read, which refactoring fits best?

options.text: Replace Nested Conditional with Guard Clauses

questions.explanation: Decompose Conditional extracts the condition and the then/else parts into named methods. Guard Clauses target a hidden normal path; Consolidate merges same-result tests; Polymorphism replaces a type-switch. (Refactoring1 p.32)

questions.stem: Given several conditional tests that all yield the same result, which refactoring fits best?
questions.explanation: Consolidate Conditional Expression combines same-result tests into one extracted conditional. Decompose extracts parts of one complex conditional; Introduce Null Object handles nulls; Polymorphism replaces type-switching. (Refactoring1 p.33)

questions.stem: Given repeated checks for null scattered through a class, which refactoring fits best?
questions.explanation: Introduce Null Object replaces repeated null checks with a do-nothing object. Polymorphism replaces type-switches; Decompose tackles one complex conditional; Hide Delegate breaks chains. (Refactoring1 p.36)

questions.stem: Given a method that both returns a value and changes object state, which refactoring restores Command-Query Separation?
questions.explanation: Separate Query from Modifier splits the dual-purpose method into a pure query and a separate modifier, restoring Command-Query Separation. The others manipulate parameters, not the query/command mix. (Refactoring1 p.38)

questions.stem: A class delegates almost every call to a held object, adding little of its own. Which smell is this, and which refactoring fixes it?

options.text: Feature Envy, fixed by Move Method
Large Class, fixed by Extract Class
Middle Man, fixed by Remove Middle Man
Message Chains, fixed by Hide Delegate

questions.explanation: Excessive delegation is the Middle Man smell, fixed by Remove Middle Man (call the delegate directly). Message Chains/Hide Delegate is the opposite tension; Feature Envy and Large Class are different smells. (Refactoring1 p.20)

questions.stem: A client navigates a.getB().getC().getD() to reach data. Which smell is this, and which refactoring breaks the chain?

options.text: Middle Man, fixed by Remove Middle Man
Data Clumps, fixed by Introduce Parameter Object
Primitive Obsession, fixed by Replace Data Value with Object
Message Chains, fixed by Hide Delegate

questions.explanation: The transitive navigation is Message Chains, broken by Hide Delegate (add server methods to hide the delegate). Middle Man/Remove Middle Man is the opposite tension; the other pairs address grouped data and primitives. (Refactoring1 p.19)

questions.stem: A subclass inherits from a superclass but overrides or ignores most of what it inherits. Which smell is this, and which refactoring is appropriate?

options.text: Refused Bequest, fixed by Replace Inheritance with Delegation
Lazy Class, fixed by Collapse Hierarchy
Parallel Inheritance Hierarchies, fixed by Tease Apart Inheritance
Large Class, fixed by Extract Class

questions.explanation: Ignoring most of an inherited contract is Refused Bequest; Replace Inheritance with Delegation removes the wrong inheritance. The other pairs match different smells. (Refactoring1 p.49)

questions.stem: Two classes do the same job but expose different method signatures for it. Which smell is this, and what is Kerievsky's pattern-directed fix?

options.text: Large Class, fixed by Extract Class
Alternative Classes with Different Interfaces, fixed by Unify Interfaces with Adapter
Duplicated Code, fixed by Form Template Method
Switch Statements, fixed by Replace Conditional with Polymorphism

questions.explanation: Differing interfaces for the same job is Alternative Classes with Different Interfaces; Kerievsky unifies them with the Adapter pattern. The other pairs match different smells/fixes. (HighLevelRefactoring p.12)

questions.stem: Kerievsky lists multiple refactorings for Long Method. Which one moves accumulation logic into a Collecting Parameter?
questions.explanation: Move Accumulation to Collecting Parameter is the named refactoring for accumulation in a Long Method. Compose Method composes small steps, Replace Conditional Dispatcher with Command uses Command, Move Accumulation to Visitor uses Visitor. (HighLevelRefactoring p.13)

questions.stem: In Kerievsky's catalog, Primitive Obsession can be refactored toward which pattern via 'Replace Type Code with Class'?

options.text: A dedicated class replacing the primitive type code

questions.explanation: Replace Type Code with Class introduces a class to replace primitive type codes — one of Kerievsky's Primitive Obsession fixes. Singleton, Observer, and Template Method are reached by other refactorings. (HighLevelRefactoring p.13)

questions.stem: A unit is 23 lines long. Which guideline does it violate and what is the prescribed action?

options.text: G1 (15 LOC) — split the unit into smaller units, e.g. with Extract Method
G2 (branch points) — replace conditionals with polymorphism

questions.explanation: 23 > 15 violates G1; the action is to split it into smaller units (Extract Method / Method Object). The other guidelines address complexity, parameters, and duplication, not raw length. (BetterCode p.7)

questions.stem: A method has 6 branch points. Which guideline does it violate, and what is its cyclomatic complexity?

options.text: G2 — CC is 7, over the limit
G2 — CC is 6, at the limit

questions.explanation: 6 branch points exceeds G2's limit of 4; CC = 6 + 1 = 7. C forgets the +1 and mis-states the limit; D/A pick the wrong guideline. (BetterCode p.16)

questions.stem: A method header declares 7 parameters. Which guideline does it violate and what is the named fix?
questions.explanation: 7 > 4 violates G4 (Keep Unit Interfaces Small); the named fix is Introduce Parameter Object. The other guidelines/fixes address length, complexity, and duplication. (BetterCode p.25)

questions.stem: Two methods share an identical 8-line block. Which guideline is violated and why?

options.text: G2 — the block adds branch points
G3 — the 8-line block exceeds the 6-line duplication threshold

questions.explanation: An 8-line duplicated block exceeds G3's >=6-line threshold, violating 'Write Code Once'. It is under 15 lines (G1 fine) and duplication is not about branch points or parameters. (BetterCode p.23)

questions.stem: A shared 5-line block appears in two methods. Does it violate Guideline 3?

options.text: No — 5 lines is below the 6-line duplication threshold
Yes — any repeated block violates G3
No — duplication is only measured at the module level

questions.explanation: G3 flags duplicated blocks of >=6 lines; a 5-line block is below threshold and does not violate it. B ignores the threshold, C uses a wrong number, D mislocates the metric (it is unit-level). (BetterCode p.23)

questions.stem: Why does BetterCode prefer small module interfaces (Guideline 5 spirit)?

options.text: Smaller interfaces increase coupling on purpose
Smaller interfaces are easier to understand, reuse, and modify
Smaller interfaces remove the need for tests

questions.explanation: Small interfaces are easier to understand, reuse, and modify (and reduce coupling). C is a runtime claim, D is false, A is the opposite of the goal. (BetterCode p.26)

questions.stem: Which G10 'How To' rule would a hard-coded number like '86400' in the middle of a method violate?
questions.explanation: A hard-coded 86400 is a magic constant, violating G10's 'no magic constants' rule (give it a named constant). Dead code, bad comments, and long identifiers are different G10 rules. (BetterCode p.58)

questions.stem: Which G10 'How To' rule does an unreachable, never-called private method violate?
questions.explanation: An unreachable, never-called method is dead code, violating G10's 'no dead code' rule. The other rules cover constants, commented-out code, and exception handling. (BetterCode p.58)

questions.stem: Which G10 'How To' rule does a large block of commented-out source code violate?
questions.explanation: Commented-out source is 'code in comments', violating that specific G10 rule (delete it; version control remembers it). 'No bad comments' covers misleading prose, 'no dead code' covers unreachable live code. (BetterCode p.58)

questions.stem: Which design pattern does JHotDraw use for its handles and tools, illustrating interchangeable behaviour?
questions.explanation: JHotDraw uses Strategy for handles/tools (interchangeable behaviour). Composite is CompositeFigure, and Singleton/Interpreter are not the handle/tool roles described. (course)

questions.stem: Which pattern does JHotDraw use when figures notify their views of changes?
questions.explanation: Figures notifying views is the Observer pattern. Decorator handles figure decorations, Adapter unifies interfaces, Template Method shares a skeleton algorithm. (course)

questions.stem: SonarLint in the lab is best described as the automated analog of what from the lecture?

options.text: The 'Symptoms of Bad Code' smells and BetterCode's metrics, run in the IDE

questions.explanation: SonarLint is the IDE-based automated analog of the smell catalog and the SIG/BetterCode metrics, detecting issues in JHotDraw. The other options are unrelated tools/concepts. (RefactLab p.1)

questions.stem: Which statement correctly captures the relationship between low-level (Fowler) and high-level (Kerievsky) refactoring?

options.text: A high-level refactoring is a sequence of low-level refactorings that introduces a design pattern
A low-level refactoring is a sequence of high-level refactorings that removes a pattern
They are identical; the labels are interchangeable
High-level refactorings change behaviour while low-level ones do not

questions.explanation: High-level/composite refactorings (Kerievsky) are sequences of low-level Fowler moves that introduce a pattern. B inverts the relationship, C denies the distinction, D wrongly claims behaviour changes (both preserve behaviour). (HighLevelRefactoring p.16)

questions.stem: Which combination of guideline, level, and threshold is entirely correct?

options.text: G4 Keep Unit Interfaces Small — Component level — 15 LOC
G1 Write Short Units — Unit level — 15 LOC
G3 Write Code Once — Module level — 4 parameters
G2 Write Simple Units — System level — 6-line duplication

questions.explanation: G1 is Unit-level with a 15-LOC threshold — fully correct. C mislabels G3's level and threshold, D mislabels G2's, A mislabels G4's — each mixes up the guideline's level and number. (BetterCode p.5,7)

questions.stem: A reviewer wants to keep both Hide Delegate and Remove Middle Man fully applied across the same relationship. Why is that impossible?

options.text: Both belong to the Big Refactorings category
They are inverses — one hides the delegate, the other exposes it; applying both fully cancels out

questions.explanation: Hide Delegate and Remove Middle Man are inverses; fully doing one undoes the other, so both cannot hold at once — you choose by dominant smell. D/A/B are false (neither is forbidden, both preserve behaviour, both are small refactorings). (Refactoring1 p.19-20)

questions.stem: Which scenario is correctly classified as NOT a refactoring?

options.text: Renaming a private variable for clarity
Extracting a 20-line block into a well-named method
Moving a method to the class whose data it uses most
Changing a method so a previously-thrown exception is no longer thrown for the same input

questions.explanation: Suppressing a previously-thrown exception changes observable behaviour, so it is a modification, not a refactoring. Renaming, Extract Method, and Move Method all preserve behaviour and are genuine refactorings. (Refactoring1 p.3)

questions.stem: Given a unit with 15 LOC, 4 branch points, 4 parameters, and no duplication, how does it fare against G1-G4?

options.text: It passes all four — each value is at (not over) its threshold
It fails G1 and G2 but passes G3 and G4
It passes G1 and G4 but fails G2 and G3

questions.explanation: 15 LOC = G1 limit, 4 branch points = G2 limit (CC 5), 4 parameters = G4 limit, no duplication = G3 fine — all at-or-under threshold, so it passes all four. The other options invent failures. (BetterCode p.10,16,23,28)
